extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A thermal incubator system comprising an insulated styrofoam container (0.03 m³ volume), a heatbed (214×214×3 mm) for uniform heating, a fan (0.02 m³/s airflow) for air circulation, three DS18S20 temperature sensors (-55°C to +125°C, 0.5°C accuracy), and a Raspberry Pi 4 Model B controller. The system regulates temperature within the insulated container, with a heatbed capable of delivering 100W power (200J in 2s) to raise air temperature from 293K (20°C) to 300K (26.85°C). The physical twin operates in a controlled environment with sensors monitoring internal and external temperatures.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Monitoring of internal/external temperatures via sensors, prediction of thermal inertia using simulation models (2-parameter and 4-parameter models), optimization of temperature control logic via the Raspberry Pi controller, and visualization of system data through RabbitMQ message brokering. The DT enables real-time simulation of the physical twin's behavior under current operating conditions, with calibration algorithms updating models and informing safety monitors.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Raspberry Pi 4 Model B as the controller with GPIO interface; DS18S20 temperature sensors using 1-Wire protocol; RabbitMQ message broker for communication between controller, drivers, and DT components; MOSFETs and optocouplers for isolating control signals from high-current circuits; PCB schematic for hardware integration; open-source GitHub repository (https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator) hosting hardware/software specifications. Simulation models (2/4-parameter) and calibration algorithms are implemented for DT model refinement.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time visualization of the incubator status is achieved by listening to controller and low-level driver messages, with data relayed to a time series database for dashboard plotting. Calibration experiments use historical or real-time data from the Physical Twin (PT) for co-simulations, and the Kalman filter processes data in real-time to estimate the heatbed state. The self-adaptation loop closes the DT loop by reconfiguring the controller based on dynamic plant behavior detected through anomalies.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The system employs two simulation models (Model A and Model B) with different parameter sets (Model A: 2 free parameters, Model B: 4 free parameters) for thermal inertia prediction. The Kalman filter uses Model B for heatbed state estimation, while Model A provides a rough approximation. The self-adaptation loop involves multiple calibration steps and reconfiguration of the Kalman filter with new parameters after experiments.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin integrates modeling, simulation, monitoring, and optimization through a centralized architecture with a Raspberry Pi controller, RabbitMQ communication server, and low-level drivers. The Kalman filter correlates measurement data with non-measured heatbed temperatures, and co-simulations relay PT data for what-if scenarios. The system orchestrates real-time data flow, calibration processes, and self-adaptation loops to maintain synchronization between physical and digital components.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The system uses RabbitMQ as an open-source message broker for inter-component communication and data exchange with external systems. The open-source repository (https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator) provides hardware/software specifications for integration with other Digital Twins or enterprise systems. The communication server enables interoperability with external monitoring tools via standardized message protocols.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Digital Twin models include behavioral and analytical models (e.g., differential equations for dynamics, thermal models with parameters C_heater, C_air, G_heater, G_box). Data sources are sensors on the Physical Twin (PT) and historical data. Data management uses time series databases and dashboards for real-time visualization. Models A and B are calibrated via nonlinear least squares optimization (Scipy package) to minimize residual error. Model B provides higher fidelity for thermal inertia prediction. Algorithms include Kalman filters for state estimation and what-if simulations using co-simulations with PT data.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators include MOSFETs for controlling 4A heatbed current, decoupled via optocouplers from Raspberry Pi GPIO. Control mechanisms use RabbitMQ for command transmission (fan/heatbed control) and state sharing. Actuation ranges are defined by thermal parameters (e.g., heater power via V*I*Δt). Safety constraints include current limits (4A) and thermal protection. Remote interfaces use RabbitMQ for real-time control and data synchronization.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are DS18S20 1-Wire Digital Thermometers (accuracy: ±0.5°C from -10°C to 85°C, range: -55°C to 125°C). Three sensors are spatially distributed for temperature monitoring. Sampling frequency is implicit via 1-Wire bus polling. Data transmission uses 1-Wire protocol at unspecified rates. Data collection mechanisms include low-level drivers interfacing with RabbitMQ for real-time telemetry.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity requires calibration of parameters (C_heater, C_air, G_heater, G_box) via experimental data comparison. Validation methods include residual error minimization (Scipy optimization) and Kalman filter-based anomaly detection. Uncertainty sources include sensor inaccuracies and model parameter errors. Error correction uses Kalman filters for heatbed state estimation. Quality assurance involves iterative calibration, model reconfiguration, and what-if simulations to validate controller optimizations.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from temperature sensors (DS18S20) to the Digital Twin (DT) via RabbitMQ communication server. Data types include temperature readings (°C) with 0.5°C accuracy. Communication protocol: AMQP (RabbitMQ). Transmission frequency: Continuous (real-time) via 1-Wire bus. Event triggers: Temperature thresholds or calibration events. Data quality assurance: Sensor calibration and filtering. Preprocessing steps: Conversion of raw sensor data to Celsius. Data integration mechanisms: RabbitMQ message queue for synchronized data ingestion.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands (temperature setpoints) are sent from DT to Raspberry Pi via RabbitMQ. Command types: Fan speed control (12V/24V), heatbed activation. Message format: JSON-based commands. Validation: Range checks on control signals. Feedback systems: Temperature sensor data streaming back to DT. Conflict resolution: Prioritizes safety constraints over user commands. Command execution confirmation: Acknowledgment via RabbitMQ delivery receipts.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication protocol: AMQP (RabbitMQ) for bidirectional data flow. Network topology: Edge (Raspberry Pi) ↔ Cloud/On-premise DT. Bandwidth: Sufficient for real-time temperature data (0.02 m³/s airflow). Latency: <2s for control loop responses. Reliability: Message persistence enabled. Redundancy: Dual temperature sensors. extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Hosting platform: Edge (Raspberry Pi 4 Model B) with optional cloud integration. Computational requirements: Raspberry Pi 4 (4-core CPU, 2GB RAM). Storage: Time-series database for temperature logs. Scalability: Modular architecture for adding sensors. Deployment models: Edge-first with cloud backup. Availability: 99.9% uptime via redundant sensor networks.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle includes development (model creation with 2 and 4 free parameters), calibration (model A/B parameter optimization), operational use (real-time monitoring via RabbitMQ), and self-adaptation (dynamic reconfiguration of Kalman filters and controllers). Transition management involves model calibration experiments and version control of simulation parameters. Governance includes integration with physical systems and continuous data-driven updates.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering processes use RabbitMQ for communication, optocouplers for signal isolation, and 1-Wire protocols for temperature sensors. Quality assurance includes calibration experiments (e.g., Model B parameters: C_air=486.12 J/K, G_box=0.856 J/K). Version control tracks model iterations (e.g., 2 vs. 4 parameter models). Evolution strategies involve re-calibrating Model B with new data and updating Kalman filters for adaptive control.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support includes Kalman filter-based anomaly detection (heatbed state estimation), what-if simulations for controller optimization (e.g., adjusting fan/heatbed parameters), and real-time dashboards. Analytics process sensor data (DS18S20) and simulation outputs. User interaction involves dashboard visualization and automated calibration triggers. Confidence measures derive from model accuracy (e.g., Model B's superior inertia prediction) and Kalman filter residuals.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Complies with open-source frameworks (RabbitMQ, GitHub repository). Follows CPS standards for cyber-physical integration [1], and uses 1-Wire protocol for sensor interoperability. Certification requirements include open-source toolchain compliance. Interoperability standards involve MQTT/AMQP for communication and IEEE 1451 for sensor networks (implied via 1-Wire).

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **6 Summary**


We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware,
software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology,
we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals.

**2.1.5** **The Temperature Sensor**

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

**3.3** **Calibration**


Although the dynamics models have been built, they contain free parameters. In order
to utilise the models, the models need to be calibrated for the incubator. The results of
the calibration are detailed in section 4.2, and here we detail the procedure.
A non-linear least squares solver was used to calibrate the parameters of the models
derived above. Then the models are evaluated using the estimated parameters. Afterwards the resulting behaviour is compared with a data trajectory that is obtained by
running an experiment of the incubator. Finally using an optimisation function inside
the python Scipy package [14] to obtain the values of the parameters. Such a function
finds the parameters that minimizes the residual error. When the residual error is small
enough, the optimised parameters can be obtained.
The principle of the package is based on least-square, it assumes that:


_y_ = _f_ ( _x, θ_ ) + _ϵ_ (10)


where _f_ ( _x, θ_ ) is the model needed to be calibrated, _θ_ are the parameters represented in
a vector in the model, and _y_ is the label or the measured data.
A cost function or an objective function can be provided as:


_J_ = �( _y_ _i_ _−_ _f_ ( _x_ _i_ _, θ_ )) [2] (11)


The objective is to acquire a minimal value of _J_, and at the same time, the corresponding _θ_ are the value needed. In order to minimise the cost function, the most commonly
used one is gradient descent. After multiple iterations, the value of the cost function
goes to a sufficient small value and the _θ_ are obtained simultaneously.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **6 Summary**


We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware,
software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology,
we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **6 Summary**


We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware,
software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology,
we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary**

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

**3.3** **Calibration**


Although the dynamics models have been built, they contain free parameters. In order
to utilise the models, the models need to be calibrated for the incubator. The results of
the calibration are detailed in section 4.2, and here we detail the procedure.
A non-linear least squares solver was used to calibrate the parameters of the models
derived above. Then the models are evaluated using the estimated parameters. Afterwards the resulting behaviour is compared with a data trajectory that is obtained by
running an experiment of the incubator. Finally using an optimisation function inside
the python Scipy package [14] to obtain the values of the parameters. Such a function
finds the parameters that minimizes the residual error. When the residual error is small
enough, the optimised parameters can be obtained.
The principle of the package is based on least-square, it assumes that:


_y_ = _f_ ( _x, θ_ ) + _ϵ_ (10)


where _f_ ( _x, θ_ ) is the model needed to be calibrated, _θ_ are the parameters represented in
a vector in the model, and _y_ is the label or the measured data.
A cost function or an objective function can be provided as:


_J_ = �( _y_ _i_ _−_ _f_ ( _x_ _i_ _, θ_ )) [2] (11)


The objective is to acquire a minimal value of _J_, and at the same time, the corresponding _θ_ are the value needed. In order to minimise the cost function, the most commonly
used one is gradient descent. After multiple iterations, the value of the cost function
goes to a sufficient small value and the _θ_ are obtained simultaneously.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **6 Summary**


We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware,
software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology,
we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals.

To do this, a DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometer (see fig. 3(e)) was used for measuring the temperature. In the incubator system, three sensors were deployed for detecting the temperature. **2.1.6** **The PCB Design** Controlling the heatbed requires approximately 4A current which is not supported by the Raspberry Pi. Instead, MOSFETs are used to separate the control signals from the Raspberry Pi and the driving currents. In order to decouple the control signals from the Raspberry Pi to the MOSFETs, we use optocouplers. The PCB schematic is shown in fig. 4. This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary**
